coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@2D CuCo-MOF composites as lubricating additives in pentaerythritol ester for enhanced tribological properties at elevated temperatures
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junhai
Tian, Dongrun
Yan, Tingting
Liang, Wenfeng
Zhang, Lixiu
Li, Xinran
Gao, Siyang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized CD@CuCo-MOF composites, which are composed of ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets of metal-organic framework (MOF) doped with carbon dots (CDs). The synthesis process involved hydrothermal and ultrasonically assisted self-assembly with a proper solvent ratio. The composites were then dispersed into pentaerythritol ester (PETE) with the aid of ultrasonic treatment. We thoroughly evaluated the physicochemical features and tribological properties of the CD@CuCo-MOF composites using advanced testing and characterization techniques including XRD, Raman, FTIR, TG, XPS, FIB-TEM and so on. The results demonstrated that the addition of CD@CuCo-MOF resulted in a 61.1 % reduction in the coefficient of friction (COF) at a concentration of 0.1 wt% when compared to the base oil at room temperature. With the rise in temperature, CD@CuCo-MOF exhibited excellent lubricating properties due to its hybrid inorganic-organic structure and interlayer sliding effect. This led to a reduction of 59.2 % and 42.9 % in the mean COF compared to PETE oil at temperatures of 100 °C and 200 °C. This enhancement in the tribological performance of CD@CuCo-MOF could be attributed to several factors, including its superior dispersion stability, excellent lubricating properties, and involvement in the formation of a tribofilm. The tribofilm formed from a combination of CDs, CuCo-MOF, metal oxides, carbides, and nitrides promoted the rearrangement of the contact surface, thereby minimizing the friction and wear between the rubbing surfaces. These findings hold significant guiding implications for advancing the development of potential-controlled lubrication using MOFs doped with CDs across a wide temperature range.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@2D CuCo-MOF composites as lubricating additives in pentaerythritol ester for enhanced tribological properties at elevated temperatures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized CD@CuCo-MOF composites, which are composed of ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets of metal-organic framework (MOF) doped with carbon dots (CDs). The synthesis process involved hydrothermal and ultrasonically assisted self-assembly with a proper solvent ratio. The composites were then dispersed into pentaerythritol ester (PETE) with the aid of ultrasonic treatment. We thoroughly evaluated the physicochemical features and tribological properties of the CD@CuCo-MOF composites using advanced testing and characterization techniques including XRD, Raman, FTIR, TG, XPS, FIB-TEM and so on. The results demonstrated that the addition of CD@CuCo-MOF resulted in a 61.1 % reduction in the coefficient of friction (COF) at a concentration of 0.1 wt% when compared to the base oil at room temperature. With the rise in temperature, CD@CuCo-MOF exhibited excellent lubricating properties due to its hybrid inorganic-organic structure and interlayer sliding effect. This led to a reduction of 59.2 % and 42.9 % in the mean COF compared to PETE oil at temperatures of 100 °C and 200 °C. This enhancement in the tribological performance of CD@CuCo-MOF could be attributed to several factors, including its superior dispersion stability, excellent lubricating properties, and involvement in the formation of a tribofilm. The tribofilm formed from a combination of CDs, CuCo-MOF, metal oxides, carbides, and nitrides promoted the rearrangement of the contact surface, thereby minimizing the friction and wear between the rubbing surfaces. These findings hold significant guiding implications for advancing the development of potential-controlled lubrication using MOFs doped with CDs across a wide temperature range.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to statistics, friction-related energy consumption constitutes over 30 % of the world’s energy sources, and the resulting wear significantly impacts the operational reliability and lifespan of mechanical components [1–3]. The use of lubricating oils is critical in minimizing friction and enhancing energy utilization efficiency, ensuring the reliability of mechanical systems. Thus, functional additives are essential components of oil that offer effective lubrication for moving mechanical components that operate under harsh conditions, including high temperatures, extreme shearing stress, and heavy loads [4–7]. Two-dimensional (2D) materials, which exemplify the new generation of superior lubrication nanomaterials, are prone to sliding in adjacent atomic interlayers. This sliding motion allows the materials to exhibit excellent lubrication performance, which is associated with the low shear resistance caused by Van Der Waals forces [8,9]. Unlike spherical nanoparticles, 2D nanomaterials with their flat-sheet-like structures offer several advantages in engineering applications. When these materials are deposited or adsorbed under the action of tribo-stress, they cover a large surface for engineering and effectively minimize friction. In addition, the strong covalent interaction among constituent atoms within a thin layer of 2D nanomaterials contributes to their exceptional stiffness and tensile strength, enhancing their ability to resist wear and sustain their performance under high levels of contact stress [10]. In the field of tribology, the use of emerging 2D materials including graphene, MoS2, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), black phosphorus, and MXene has gained momentum due to their unique anti-friction capabilities [11–17].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a type of crystalline coordination polymers that predominantly consist of either inorganic metal ions or organic bridging ligands. At present, MOFs have found broad applications in various fields including energy conversion, catalysis, microwave absorption, sensors, and electrochemistry [18–24]. Additionally, the tribology community has shown a growing interest in MOFs with hybrid inorganic and organic structures. These materials have garnered attention due to their flexible skeleton framework, exceptional chemical stability, and large surface area [25]. Yu et al. synthesized 2D MOFs nanosheets as 500 N oil additives using an ultrasonically assisted self-assembly method, and investigated the tribological performance of these materials [26]. Their findings revealed that the 0.1 wt% Ni-MOF could remarkably reduce the coefficient of friction (COF) and demonstrated excellent wear resistance. These outstanding tribological properties were attributed to the interlayer sliding effect, the polishing effect, and the generation of a tribofilm. Zhu et al. designed the MOF-on-MOF heterostructure of ZIF-7/2D Ni-BDC and incorporated the as-prepared samples into liquid paraffin [27]. The research demonstrated that this unique “ball-on-plate” heterostructure induced a synergistic interaction between rolling and interlayered sliding during the friction process. As a result, the addition of the prepared samples at 0.08 wt% led to reductions of 39.7 % and 18.03 % in the COF and wear scar diameter, respectively, compared to pure liquid paraffin. Cheng’s group successfully synthesized 2D Ni/Fe-MOF nanosheets, which were then added into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogels to enhance their mechanical and tribological properties [28]. The 2D Ni-Fe/PVA composite hydrogel with a filling content of 0.15 wt% showed a COF 41 % lower compared to pure PVA hydrogel. This reduction could be attributed to the interlayer structure present in 2D Ni/Fe-MOF nanosheets. Luo et al. prepared five MOFs characterized by a square grid structure and employed experimental results analysis and density functional theory (DFT) simulation to identify the potential lubricating mechanisms involved [29]. Superlubricity was achieved, with the corresponding COF being around 5.3 × 10-4. This can be explained by the ligand’s high degree of conjugation and the elimination of the puckering effect. In summary, various factors including size, thickness, microstructure, and surface chemistry all played important roles in the lubrication effect [25,30].

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, are increasingly recognized as highly promising candidates for addressing many scientific and technological problems in fields including aerospace, manufacturing, medicine, sensing, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [31–34]. This is primarily due to their advantageous characteristics, such as distinctive optical properties, controllable functionalized surface, physicochemical stability, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, and remarkable biocompatibility. In recent years, there has been a growing interest from the tribology community towards carbon dots (CDs) due to their unique attributes, including their small and uniform size (<10 nm), excellent tunability, and abundant copious groups. These characteristics make CDs well-suited for serving as additives that enhance wear resistance and reduce friction [35]. Mou et al. utilized an H2O2-assisted solvothermal route to synthesize CDs with uniform distribution, ultrasmall size, and abundant surface groups [36]. Their research revealed that when used as additives in PEG200, CDs exhibited noteworthy lubricating properties, including exceptional lubricity, enhanced load-carrying capacity, and prolonged operational lifespan in conditions of boundary lubrication. To enhance dispersion stability and lubricating property, Wang’s group prepared CDs modified with ionic liquids (IL) [37]. According to the findings of the study, the lubricating mechanism of CDs-IL could be attributed to the combined lubricating effects of the CDs. These effects include the film lubrication provided by the IL groups, as well as the nano-lubrication of the carbon cores, which encompasses actions such as rolling, mending, and polishing. As a result, the CDs-IL exhibited outstanding properties in reducing friction and preventing wear. In addition, researchers have incorporated diverse heteroatoms, including nitrogen, sulfur, fluorine, and metallic elements, into the structure of CDs. The CDs doped with these heteroatoms have displayed favorable lubrication performance. Tang et al. fabricated Ni-CDs particles as additives in PEG oil using a hydrothermal method, and found that the addition of Ni-CDs additives endowed the oil film with an enhanced load-carrying capacity, which in turn led to reduced friction and wear [38]. Wang et al. verified that the use of Ag-CDs as high-performance lubricating additives yielded improved friction reduction and wear-resistance properties in PAO oil, specifically at elevated temperatures [39]. The potential lubricating mechanisms were assigned to the involvement of Ag-CDs additives in the formation of a tribofilm, thereby promoting their lubrication effect. Zhang et al. synthesized CDs doped with boron and nitrogen (B/N) elements in order to enhance the lubrication performance of PEG [40]. Their findings revealed that the addition of such additives at 1 wt% could improve the antiwear and friction reduction properties of PEG by 89.34 % and 62.18 %, respectively. The enhancement of lubrication can be attributed to a specific mechanism whereby the CDs undergo a transformation and form organized carbon materials that cover the worn surface. This transformation effectively prevents undesirable wear and reduces friction. In summary, the lubrication performance of CDs can be influenced by several factors, including surface passivation, a spherical carbon core structure, ultrasmall size, excellent adsorption capacity, the micro-ball rolling effect, and the formation of a tribofilm [35,41,42].

If the 2D MOF combined with carbon dots to create a type of 0D/2D composites, the potential advantage in lubrication could be made. Firstly, the addition of carbon dots could improve the embedability of 2D MOF in the worn area. Secondly, the combination could restrain self-aggregation of the nanoadditives, which could promote the dispersion stability in the base fluid. Thirdly, the composites could be expected to generate a tribofilm under the shear and friction heat, which was beneficial to the friction-minimizing and wear-resistance. In this study, CD@CuCo-MOF hybrid composites were synthesized using facial hydrothermal and self-assembly methods, and were then dispersed in pentaerythritol ester (PETE) base oil through ultrasonic treatment. The morphology and structure of these composites were thoroughly investigated through a series of characterizations. The performance of these composites as oil additives was investigated in terms of tribology. This investigation involved conducting tests using a ball-on-disk reciprocating configuration at different temperatures. Detailed discussions were carried out regarding the lubricating mechanisms of CD@CuCo-MOF additives based on friction tests and characterizations of the worn surface.

Citric acid (CA, A.R., ≥99 %) and diethylenetriamine were procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Copper nitrate hexahydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, A.R., ≥98 %) and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, A.R., ≥99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Terephthalic acid (1, 4-H2BDC, C.P., ≥98.5 %), N-N dimethylformamide (DMF, C.P., ≥99 %), triethylamine (TEA, C.P., ≥99 %), and ethanol were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. PETE synthetic oil was acquired from Shenyang Hongcheng Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. Notably, all of the reagents and chemicals were utilized as received with no further treatment.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation process of the CD@CuCo-MOF composites. The detailed procedure for preparing CDs was as follows: A mixture consisting of 1.5 g of CA, 1.5 mL of diethylenetriamine, and 20 mL of distilled water was stirred using a glass rod until a transparent solution was obtained. The solution was then transferred to a reaction kettle and subjected to a reaction at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the solution underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to collect supernatant. The obtained supernatant was subsequently filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and subjected to 24 h of dialysis using a 500 Da dialysis bag to eliminate small molecular impurities. The water used during the dialysis process was changed every 3 h to ensure more efficient removal of impurities. To achieve high-quality CDs, the solvent was subjected to freeze drying for a duration of 24 h.

The CD@CuCo-MOF composites were prepared using a facial self-assembly method. To begin with, a mixed solution consisting of 64 mL of DMF, 4 mL of ethanol, and 4 mL of deionized water was prepared. Into this mixed solution, 1.5 mmol of H2BDC was added. Then, 2 mL of 3 mg/mL CD solution was added through ultrasonic treatment. Under stirring, the mixed solution was supplemented with 0.75 mmol of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 0.75 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O until both the copper salt and cobalt salt were fully dissolved. Afterwards, a homogeneous colloidal suspension was achieved by adding 0.8 mL of TEA into the mixed solution while stirring vigorously. Then, the colloidal suspension was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 3 h under sealed conditions and left to stand for 12 h. The resulting solids were obtained through centrifugation and washed alternately with deionized water and ethanol to completely eliminate unreacted substances. Finally, the CD@CuCo-MOF composites were obtained by subjecting them to vacuum drying at 60 °C for a duration of 12 h. The CuCo-MOF composites were prepared using a similar synthesis method to allow for comparison.

The PETE synthetic oil was selected as the base lubricating oil due to its excellent physicochemical properties, which are outlined in Table S1. The as-prepared CD@CuCo-MOF additives, each with a different weight, were added into the PETE base oil while stirring. Then, the oil mixtures underwent ultrasonication for 2 h to achieve homogeneous suspension liquids.

The reciprocal Rtec MFT5000 multifunctional friction tester was used to assess the tribological performance of the oil mixtures. The friction tests employed commercially available Si3N4 balls with a hardness of around 1700 Hv and a diameter of 6.35 mm as the stationary upper counterparts. The lower specimens used in the tests were GH4169 superalloys measuring 25 mm × 25 mm × 4 mm. These superalloys had a hardness of around 85HRC and a chemical composition consisting of ≤ 0.1 wt% Co, ≤0.08 wt% C, 2.8–3.3 wt% Mo, 0.3–0.7 wt% Al, 0.75–1.15 wt% Ti, 17-21 wt% Cr, 50–55 wt% Ni, and balance Fe. The surface roughness of these specimens was limited to a maximum of 20 nm. The friction tests were conducted under specific conditions, including an applied load of 40 N, a sliding frequency of 2 Hz, an amplitude of 10 mm, and a testing duration of 15 min. The temperatures for the test were set at 25 °C, 100 °C, and 200 °C, respectively. Before the friction tests were conducted, the friction pairs underwent a 15-min ultrasonic cleaning using acetone to eliminate any contamination. Each friction test was performed three times under the exact same conditions so as to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the data.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XDR7000) analysis was performed to determine the phase composition of the as-prepared samples. The analysis involved utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) and scanning over a range of 2θ from 5° to 90° at a speed of 4°/min. Using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Thermo Scientific Talos F200S) that operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, the morphologies and microstructures of the as-prepared samples were examined. An STA 449 F5 thermal analyzer was used to conduct the thermogravimetric analysis. The analysis was performed under an Ar atmosphere, with a heating speed of 10 °C/min, and a temperature range from 25 °C to 700 °C. Additionally, the functional groups present in the as-prepared samples were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR, Nocole iS5). The characterization covered the wavenumber range of 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. A Raman microscope (Horiba, LabRam HR Evolution) that features a 638 nm laser for excitation was used to examine the composition of the CD@CuCo-MOF samples. The chemical states of the typical elements in the CD@CuCo-MOF samples were determined using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, TA, ESCALAB250) that features a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray radioactive source (1486.6 eV). The C1s peak observed at 284.6 eV was taken as a reference for calibration. A Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano ZS90 instrument (Malvern Instruments, UK) was employed to measure the Zeta potentials of the samples. A video-based optical system (Dataphysics OCA 20) was utilized to observe the contact angles of the oil with and without different lubricating additives on the superalloys. The morphologies and composition of the worn surfaces were examined using an S4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) that features an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The worn surfaces were analyzed to measure their 2D profile and 3D wear morphologies using an Rtec 3D surface profilometer (Rtec, Ups-sigma). A FLIR A6XX infrared thermal imaging device was used to quantify the temperature changes in the lubricating medium. Using a focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM) (Thermo Scientific Helios 5 CX), the cross-section of the alloy’s worn surface was successfully extracted. The microstructure of the tribofilm was analyzed using TEM and EDS. Moreover, the existing state and composition of the worn surface were identified through Raman and XPS characterizations.

Fig. 2 a displays the XRD patterns of the obtained powders. It could be observed that the as-prepared CDs showed a wide diffraction peak at 23.4°, which corresponded to the (002) lattice plane. The diffraction peaks observed in the CuCo-MOF powders were consistent with the findings reported in the existing literature [43]. The CD@CuCo-MOF composites exhibited similar XRD patterns to CuCo-MOF, and the reason for this might be that the amount of carbon dots exposed on the spot of the X-ray was too little to be detected. Meanwhile, this also suggested the inserted CDs did not affect the structural characteristics of CuCo-MOF. Fig. 2b illustrates the Raman spectrums of CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF. The presence of peaks at 1610 cm−1 and 1135 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of a benzene ring with an organic linker as well as the deprotonated CO species in the carboxylic acid groups, respectively [44]. The peaks at 1425 cm−1 and 855 cm−1 were associated with the CH bonds of the benzene ring [45]. The two weak peaks observed at 443 cm−1 and 631 cm−1 were assigned to the CuO and CoO band vibrations, respectively. The CDs’ characteristic peaks at 1353 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 were detected in the heteroatom-doped CD@CuCo-MOF, suggesting the successful incorporation of CDs through the creation of hybrid composites. The FTIR spectra of CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF are shown in Fig. 2c. For the CD@CuCo-MOF composites, the infrared absorption peaks at approximately 3420 cm−1 and 3121 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively [46]. The appearance of absorption peaks at 1575 cm−1, 1390 cm−1, and 1017 cm−1 were associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of coordination carboxyl groups in the H2BDC ligand [47]. The characteristic peaks at 1498 cm−1 and 1148 cm−1 corresponded to the presence of the benzene ring in the H2BDC ligand [48]. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1103 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching bond of the CO group, while the characteristic peak at 556 cm−1 was assigned to the metal-O coordination bond [49]. In the case of CDs, the stretching vibration peaks at 1711 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, and 1391 cm−1 were observed, which were attributed to the presence of the CO, CC, and COOH groups, respectively. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was employed to evaluate the thermal stability of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2d, the weight loss of all the samples was found to take place at temperatures exceeding 230 °C, indicating the removal of functional groups in the sample. Hence, the TG analysis revealed that the three types of as-prepared additives possessed remarkable thermal stability. To further determine the chemical states of some typical elements in the as-prepared samples, XPS characterization was conducted. The detailed XPS spectra of certain typical elements in the CD@CuCo-MOF composites are displayed in Fig. 2e-i. The C1s signal was fitted into three characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to the CC, COH/CN, and COOH bonds, respectively [19]. The binding energy of O1s at 531.3 eV and 532.2 eV was associated with the metal-O and OCO bonds, respectively [50]. The characteristic peak of N1s at 401.8 eV was attributed to the CN bond. The signals in Cu2p at 934.5 eV and 941.9 eV were associated with the presence of Cu2+ and Cu+ ions [51]. The characteristic peaks of Co2p at 780.9 eV and 782.3 eV were attributed to the presence of Co3+ and Co2+ ions [52].

Fig. 3 presents the TEM images and corresponding EDS mapping results of the CD@CuCo-MOF hybrid composites. These composites maintained a layered nanosheet structure, where numerous CDs with uniform size were well dispersed across the surface of CuCo-MOF nanosheets (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution TEM image displays a distinct lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm, which corresponded to the lattice spacing (002) in the graphite carbon structure [53] (Fig. 3b). Additionally, the elemental mapping analysis suggested that the CD@CuCo-MOF composites consisted of Cu, Co, C, N, and O elements, which were evenly distributed throughout the structure (Fig. 3c).

To unlock the maximum tribological potential, it is vital for lubricating additives to maintain exceptional dispersion stability [54]. Fig. 4 a shows the optical images of the oil mixtures that were left to stand for different periods of time. Observations revealed that no visible aggregates or stratification were detected after a standing time of 7 days. This indicated that the CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF exhibited excellent dispersion stability in PETE base oil following the ultrasonic treatment. The findings from the contact angle analysis of the oil mixtures on the superalloy are displayed in Fig. 4b. It could be observed that the contact angles of PETE with the additives were lower compared to pure PETE oil. This indicated that the three types of additives could enhance the wetting properties of the base oil on metal alloys. The CD@CuCo-MOF additives exhibited the lowest contact angle of 34.5° when in contact with oil droplets. This could be explained by the interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups in the CDs and MOF with the polar groups in PETE oil, which enhanced the dispersion ability and compatibility of the CD@CuCo-MOF in the polar PETE oil. Consequently, the outstanding wetting properties of the lubricating additives on the metal surface contributed significantly to the superior lubricity of the oil mixtures. The Zeta potential measurements revealed that the CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF had surface charges of –32.3 eV, −31.1 eV, and −34.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 4c). Generally, a greater absolute value of Zeta potential signified better dispersion stability for a nano-suspension system under the same condition [55]. Thus, the introduction of doped CDs with a negative charge could create electrostatic hindrance and prevent the agglomeration between 2D MOFs. Meanwhile, the presence of MOFs nanosheets helped reduce the agglomeration of CDs. In addition, the lubricating additive with a negative charge facilitated its own deposition on the metal surface. This phenomenon could be attributed to the emission of low-energy electrons from the worn surface during friction, resulting in a positively charged metal surface [36]. According to the aforementioned findings, the CD@CuCo-MOF additives possessed remarkable dispersibility in PETE oil when exposed to ultrasonic processing, increasing their susceptibility to adsorption on the worn surface.

To evaluate the tribological performance of CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF additives in PETE oil, a reciprocating ball-on-disk contact configuration was utilized. The study also examined the effects of additive contents and environmental temperatures on the lubricating properties of these additives. To begin with, the tribological performance of oil containing CD@CuCo-MOF additives at various concentrations was evaluated under room temperature. The coefficient of friction (COF) curves and mean COF values are presented in Fig. 5 . As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the COF value of pure PETE oil rapidly increased and reached approximately 0.134 in a short period of time. Then, the COF value gradually decreased and stabilized around 0.081, and the mean COF value of the base oil remained at 0.089 throughout the entire friction test. It was evident that the addition of CD@CuCo-MOF additives at different concentrations (0.05 wt%, 0.08 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.15 wt%, and 0.2 wt%) led to a reduction in the COF value of the base oil to varying degree. Among the samples, the concentration of 0.1 wt% exhibited the lowest COF compared to the others. The COF values of the oil with a concentration of 0.15 wt% or 0.2 wt% were higher compared to the oil with a concentration of 0.1 wt%, but they remained lower than the COF value of the base oil. The reason for this could be the fact that excessive additives may disrupt the continuous supply of the oil mixture, resulting in a decrease in the load-carrying capacity of the oil film in local contact areas [56].

The tribological performance of oil with different additives at a concentration of 0.1 wt% was examined under elevated temperatures. The resulting COF curves are presented in Fig. 6 , and the repeated COF experimental results are displayed in Fig.S1. It was noticeable that as the temperature increased, the COF values of the base oil also increased, eventually reaching a maximum value of 0.202 at 200 °C. This occurrence could be attributed to the decline in the viscosity of the base oil as the temperature increased. Consequently, the load-carrying capacity of the oil film decreased, resulting in direct contact between the rubbing surfaces in certain regions. At elevated temperatures, the COF values for all three types of additives were significantly reduced compared to the base oil. Specifically, at a temperature of 200 °C, the mean COF of the oil with CD@CuCo-MOF additives was approximately 0.115, representing a remarkable reduction of 42.9 % compared to the COF of the base oil. It could be observed that the COF curves of oil with different additives fluctuated at run-in stage, and the reason for this might be that the formation of the stable tribofilm needed time under the shear and friction heat at such high temperature. Additionally, compared to the COF values of oil with individual CDs additives or CuCo-MOF additives, the oil with CD@CuCo-MOF additives exhibited the shortest run-in stage characterized by an unstable and fluctuating friction curve, and the oil with CD@CuCo-MOF additives demonstrated the lowest COF value at 200 °C. Hence, the CD@CuCo-MOF hybrid composites had excellent tribological performance at elevated temperatures. This could be explained by the synergistic lubrication effect achieved through the combination of CDs and CuCo-MOF sheets in the composites. Some carbon dots additives with ultrasmall size quickly penetrated into worn area to repair the tiny pits and valley, resulting in a smooth worn surface. The CuCo-MOF sheets with large area and shear-susceptible feature covered the larger contact interfaces to prevent the rubbing pair from direct contact. Furthermore, the introduction of carbon dots enhanced the embedability of CuCo-MOF into the worn area and generated a robust protective film. The COF curves of the oil with varying concentrations of CD@CuCo-MOF additives at elevated temperatures are displayed in Fig.S2. The concentration of 0.1 wt% possessed the lowest COF values at 100 °C and 200 °C respectively compared with those of the rest concentrations, suggesting that the 0.1 wt% concentration was also the optimum content at elevated temperatures.

It is widely acknowledged that the consumption of friction-induced energy in reciprocating mode will be converted into heat at the contact interface, resulting in a rise in temperature for both the rubbing surfaces and the lubricating material. Table 1 presents the temperature changes of the oil with different lubricating additives after undergoing sliding friction at room temperature. Compared to the other samples, the base oil showed the most significant change in temperature, with a rise of approximately 29.2 °C. The temperature changes observed in the oil containing 0.1 wt% CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF additives were smaller than those in the base oil. Notably, the CD@CuCo-MOF suspension exhibited the lowest rise in temperature, aligning with the COF results. This phenomenon could be explained by the deposition of an appropriate concentration of CD@CuCo-MOF additives on the worn surface [39]. These additives possess high thermal conductivity, allowing them to effectively dissipate the heat generated by friction. As a result, the adhesive attraction between the rubbing surfaces decreased, leading to a reduction in friction.

Besides COFs, wear is another important factor for evaluating tribological performance. Fig. 7 illustrates the 2D profiles and 3D morphologies of worn surfaces lubricated with different oil mixtures at elevated temperatures. At a temperature of 200 °C, the worn surface lubricated with the base oil exhibited the largest width (897.7 μm) and depth (30.6 μm), demonstrating severe wear. In contrast, when lubricated with oil containing CDs, CuCo-MOF, and CD@CuCo-MOF additives, the width and depth of the worn tracks decreased to 694.4 μm and 23.5 μm, 793.4 μm and 25.4 μm, and 567.5 μm and 16.1 μm, respectively. Similarly, at a temperature of 100 °C, the cross-sections of the wear areas lubricated with oil that contained additives were found to be smaller than those lubricated with PETE alone. This observation indicated the superior lubricating capabilities of the three types of additives at elevated temperatures. Fig.S3 presents the 2D profiles and 3D morphologies of worn surfaces lubricated with different oil mixtures at room temperature. Strikingly, the CD@CuCo-MOF additives showcased the highest wear-resistance capacity under the same condition. Generally, local asperity contacts lead to accumulative plastic deformations that render the material on the subsurface susceptible to shear instabilities, resulting in surface roughening and plowing [57]. As a result, the COF of the contact surface would increase, aggravating wear. However, the inclusion of CD@CuCo-MOF additives could effectively repair the worn surface and mitigate plastic deformation in local asperity areas. This, in turn, would contribute to reduced friction and wear. Fig.S4 displays the optical images of wear scars on the upper Si3N4 ball lubricated with oil containing various additives at elevated temperatures. Under the same condition, the base oil possessed the largest wear scar diameter, indicating a decline in its lubricating properties as the temperature increased. In contrast, the inclusion of the three types of additives resulted in a certain reduction in wear scar diameter, with the oil containing CD@CuCo-MOF additives exhibiting the smallest wear scar diameter among all the samples. This observation highlighted the exceptional lubricating effect of CD@CuCo-MOF additives.

Fig. 8 presents the SEM images of the alloys’ worn surfaces when they were lubricated with various lubricating samples at elevated temperatures. It was evident from the images that the worn surfaces lubricated with PETE base oil exhibited notable damage(Fig. 8a and b). This damage was characterized by deep plows and delaminations caused by considerable plastic deformations. This implied that the worn surface underwent severe wear. This could be explained by the decreased load-carrying capacity of the oil film with decreased oil viscosity at elevated temperatures. Consequently, direct contact between the rubbing surfaces occurred at local interfaces. The occurrence of such local direct contact would bring about serious wear, resulting in the generation of significant debris and pits. This further exacerbated surface damage, indicating the inferior properties of the base oil in terms of wear resistance and friction reduction at elevated temperatures. The breakdown of the contact interface would trigger the formation of defects and microcracks on both the surface and subsurface. This allowed oxygen to diffuse, leading to the generation of a metal oxide layer [58]. This metal oxide layer was characterized by its hardness and brittleness, making it prone to being peeled off when in contact with the Si3N4 counterpart, which had a higher hardness. Meanwhile, the friction process involved the accumulation of wear debris and oxide scale, causing an increase in the COF. In contrast, the worn surfaces that were lubricated with oil that contained additives exhibited a relatively smooth appearance, with shallow furrows and mild plastic transformations. Additionally, there was a reduction in the size of the pits on the worn surface, and only minimal wear debris could be observed. Under the same experimental condition, the worn surfaces lubricated with oil containing CD@CuCo-MOF exhibited the shallowest and smoothest worn surfaces. The EDS results revealed the presence of C, O, N, Cu, Co, Ni, Fe, and Cr elements on the worn surface that was lubricated with oil that contained CD@CuCo-MOF(Fig. 8g and h). This suggested that the CD@CuCo-MOF additives were absorbed onto the worn surface. A possible explanation for this SEM and EDS characterization was that a sequence of complex tribochemical reactions took place between the base oil, CD@CuCo-MOF additives, and metal oxides. This resulted in the formation of a protective tribofilm, which effectively minimized direct contact between the rubbing surfaces.

To further understand the lubricating mechanism of CD@CuCo-MOF additives in PETE oil at 200 °C, the TEM images and element distributions for the tribofilm’s cross-sections(Fig. 9 ), which were obtained using FIB technology(Fig. 9a), are presented to allow for direct observation of the microstructures of the tribofilm and determination of its chemical composition. Three distinct parts can be identified, and the upper part was the capped layer, consisting of a tungsten element, which served to protect the tribofilm from damage during sample preparation. The lower part represented the metal substrate, while the tribofilm was situated in the middle part (Fig. 9b and c). The tribofilm on the substrate was observed to be dense and continuous, with a thickness of around 41.6 nm. According to the HAADF-STEM analysis(Fig. 9d), the presence of C, O, N, Cu, Co, Ni, Fe, and Cr elements was detected in the tribofilm. This confirmed that the tribofilm was composed of CD@CuCo-MOF additives and certain metal oxides. These metal oxides served as hard phases, offering structural support, and the CD@CuCo-MOF additives and their derivatives were distributed around the metal oxides, playing a lubricating role. Thus, as revealed by the tribofilm’s composition and structure, a tribochemical reaction took place on the alloy’s surface during the friction process. The resulting complex reaction products combined with CD@CuCo-MOF additives to form a protective tribofilm. The presence of this tribofilm contributed to improved friction-minimizing performance and wear resistance.

Raman spectra was employed to analyze the composition of the worn surfaces lubricated with pure oil or oil containing CDs, CuCo-MOF, CD@CuCo-MOF additives at 200 °C. As shown in Fig. 10 , the characteristic peaks of the worn surface lubricated with the base oil at 831 cm−1 and 698 cm−1 were assigned to the presence of metal oxides such as NiO and iron oxides. This suggested that a tribochemical reaction took place during the process. In contrast, when CD@CuCo-MOF additives were introduced to the oil, the Raman spectra exhibited two characteristic peaks at around 1349 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1, which corresponded to the D band and G band of the CDs. The relative intensity ratio of ID/IG was about 1.03. This could be attributed to doped CDs with a graphene-like structure that underwent a shift towards disordering along with an increased number of wrinkles and defects under friction-induced heat and shear. This observation suggested the involvement of CDs in the formation of the tribofilm. Additionally, as the temperature increased, there was an inclination towards higher levels of disorder and defects, which proved beneficial for the lubrication performance at elevated temperatures. Meanwhile, when the worn surface was lubricated with oil containing CD@CuCo-MOF additives, two characteristic peaks were detected at 1419 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1, which corresponded to the MOF structure. Consequently, reciprocal shear gave rise to an increased number of defects and a higher level of disorder in the structure. Due to the susceptibility to shear of the carbon with MOFs, a stable tribofilm was formed on the worn surface. This tribofilm effectively isolated straight asperity contact, resulting in improved lubrication performance.

Fig. 11 illustrates the detailed XPS spectra of several typical elements found on the worn surface lubricated with PETE that contained 0.1 wt% CD@CuCo-MOF additives at a temperature of 200 °C. An XPS signal corresponding to C1s was fitted into three distinct peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.2 eV, and 287.4 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO bonds, respectively. In the O1s spectra, three different peaks were observed at around 530.4 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.5 eV, which could be assigned to metal-O, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. The Cu2p peaks at 934.5 eV and 940.2 eV were associated with the presence of Cu2+ and Cu+ ions. The Co2p signals observed at 780.7 eV and 782.0 eV corresponded to the existence of Co3+ and Co2+ ions. A signal corresponding to N1s was detected on the worn surface, with the characteristic peak at 401.5 eV being ascribed to the C-N bond. Moreover, the signal detected at 855.1 eV in the Ni2p spectra was linked to NiO. The emergence of Fe2p peaks at 710.4 eV and 712.1 eV indicated the presence of iron oxides. The intense peak observed at 575.9 eV in the Cr2p spectra could be attributed to the Cr3+ in Cr2O3. The detection of metal oxides containing Ni, Fe, and Cr elements verified the occurrence of tribo-oxidation during the friction process. The oxygen adjacent to the contact interface quickly diffused into the rubbed surface, along with the formation of micro-cracks and defects caused by shear and frictional heat. This oxygen then reacted with the metallic elements of the superalloy, resulting in the formation of a glaze layer composed of metal oxides. XPS analysis suggested that the tribochemical products underwent complex reactions and transformations during friction, eventually forming a tribofilm that contained the CD@CuCo-MOF additive, metal oxides, nitrides, and carbides. Such a tribofilm could efficiently avoid direct contact between the rubbing surfaces, thereby lowering friction and wear.

According to the above analysis, the lubricating mechanism of CD@CuCo-MOF as oil additives at elevated temperatures could be summarized as depicted in Fig. 12 . Firstly, ensuring the appropriate concentration of CD@CuCo-MOF additives with excellent dispersion stability in PETE was essential in enabling the oil mixture to continuously reach the contact surface of the friction pair. This step was crucial for the oil mixture to carry out its lubricating function. Secondly, it was observed that low-energy electrons tended to be released from the microconvexities present on the friction surfaces, and this led to a positive charge buildup on the rubbed interfaces. Under the action of shear, some negatively charged CDs were released and readily adsorbed onto the worn surface, creating a seamless and durable adsorption film. Meanwhile, the ultrathin 2D structure of MOFs was ground into small pieces. These pieces could easily embed themselves within the groove area, facilitating the repair and polishing of the friction interface, resulting in a reduction in roughness and wear. Finally, as a result of the frictional heat and shear, a complex tribochemical reaction took place between the metal, oxygen, and the CD@CuCo-MOF additives. This reaction led to the generation of a tribochemical reaction film comprising metal oxides, CDs, MOFs, carbides, and nitrides. The presence of this film effectively alleviated the direct contact between the rubbing surfaces. Therefore, the inherent susceptibility to sliding, polishing capabilities, repair functionality, and the generation of a tribofilm comprising metal oxides collectively contributed to the excellent friction-reducing and wear-resistant properties exhibited by CD@CuCo-MOF.

In this study, we successfully synthesized 0D/2D hybrid composites of CD@CuCo-MOF using facial hydrothermal and self-assembly methods. These composites were then steadily dispersed into PETE base oil through ultrasonic treatment. The physicochemical features and tribological properties of the CD@CuCo-MOF composites were thoroughly investigated using XRD, TEM, FTIR, TG, Raman, and XPS characterizations. Under the influence of ultrasonic treatment, the CD@CuCo-MOF additives maintained excellent dispersion stability in the PETE oil for seven days without any evident precipitation. At room temperature, the optimal concentration of these additives was found to be 0.1 wt%, resulting in lower friction coefficients. Specifically, the lowest friction coefficient was 0.035, which represented a reduction of 61.1 % compared to the friction coefficient of the base oil. At elevated temperatures, it was manifested that the CD@CuCo-MOF suspension could effectively improve the friction-minimizing and wear-resistance properties of the base oil, and possessed COF value of 0.059 and 0.115 at 100 °C and 200 °C, respectively. This improvement could be primarily attributed to the occurrence of complex tribochemical reactions during friction. Consequently, a robust tribofilm composed of CDs, MOF, metal oxides, and complex carbides and nitrides formed on the sliding surface, and this tribofilm played a crucial role in significantly reducing friction and wear at elevated temperatures.
